ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Plaster Prata or roti prata with egg. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'flatbread (food product)' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Flat bread) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for roti prata or plaster prata in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for plaster prata or roti prata as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of plaster prata is approximately 130 g, equivalent to one piece of egg prata as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or Indian Muslim restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Plaster Prata — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Plaster Prata (cr_id G00010312). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for roti canai/paratha (USDA SR Legacy #18364) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: Roti (Indian flatbread, white wheat flour) — Glycemic Index analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Plaster Prata specifically. GI of 62 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain roti/Indian flatbread made from white wheat flour (GI ~62, medium category). The egg topping does not substantially alter the GI of the bread base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: net carbohydrate per serving = (75.23 − 10.2) × 1.30 = 84.5 g; GL = 62 × 84.5 / 100 = 52.4. Note: some references report plain roti GI as 62 ± 3 (medium). This estimate is conservative and appropriate for clinical guidance.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Plaster prata is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to its sodium content of 632 mg/100 g; patients should limit consumption to occasional small portions, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid high-sodium curry dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (75.2 g/100 g) partially offset by high dietary fibre (10.2 g/100 g) and egg protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit plaster prata to one piece per meal, pair it with a protein-rich curry to moderate postprandial glucose response, and avoid sweet condiments such as sugar syrup or condensed milk that are sometimes served alongside.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (632 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g estimated) with low cholesterol (0 mg/100 g per HPB)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, plaster prata should be consumed in moderation due to its high sodium content which contributes to blood pressure elevation; requesting oil-based cooking instead of ghee reduces saturated fat intake, and pairing with a vegetable-based curry improves the overall dietary profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, promoting tissue fluid retention and worsening joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plaster prata is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium content of 632 mg/100 g; if consumed, request reduced-salt preparation, substitute ghee with vegetable oil to lower saturated fat, and pair with a turmeric-rich curry for anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plaster prata is a popular Singaporean Indian hawker dish consisting of a flaky, layered wheat-flour flatbread cooked on a hot griddle with a sunny-side-up egg (mata lembu) pressed directly onto the surface during cooking, giving it its distinctive 'plaster' appearance. The dough is made from white wheat flour, water, salt, and ghee or margarine, which is folded and stretched repeatedly to create thin, crispy layers. The egg is cracked onto the prata while it cooks, resulting in a golden, lacy-edged flatbread with a set egg on top. Plaster prata is typically served with a side of fish or chicken curry for dipping, and sometimes with sugar or condensed milk as a sweet alternative. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 412 kcal, 9.85 g protein, 8.25 g fat, and 75.23 g carbohydrate, with notably high dietary fibre at 10.2 g/100 g and elevated sodium at 632 mg/100 g. The high sodium content makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis. The high carbohydrate load requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high fibre content partially moderates glycaemic response. The egg contributes additional protein and micronutrients. Plaster prata is a staple breakfast and supper item at Indian Muslim (mamak) stalls across Singapore and is culturally significant as part of the broader roti prata family, which includes plain prata, egg prata, cheese prata, and tissue prata.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Plaster Prata (cr_id G00010312) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy roti/paratha analogue. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous flatbread entry. All mandatory multilingual names present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roti/paratha (closest approved-source analogue) as the HPB Foodo record does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, most vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for plaster prata are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB source; this is noted as potentially anomalous given the presence of a sunny-side-up egg — the HPB value is preserved as reported and flagged for review. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (cr_id G00010312). 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain Indian flatbread/roti made from white wheat flour; no direct GI measurement for plaster prata was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving (130 g) calculated as: net carbohydrate per serving = (75.23 − 10.2) × 1.30 = 84.5 g; GL = 62 × 84.5 / 100 = 52.4. 5. The input record lists cholesterol as 0.0 mg/100 g which is preserved from HPB; this may reflect the whole-dish composite average rather than the egg component alone.
